theorem_title: Quantifier elimination for dense linear orders without endpoints
theorem_statement:
Let \(T_{\mathrm{DLO}}\) be the first-order theory in the language \({<}\) whose models are dense linear orders without endpoints. That is, \(T_{\mathrm{DLO}}\) says:

1. \(<\) is a strict linear order;
2. density: if \(a<b\), then there exists \(c\) such that \(a<c<b\);
3. no endpoints: for every \(a\), there exist \(b,c\) such that \(b<a<c\).

Then \(T_{\mathrm{DLO}}\) admits quantifier elimination: for every first-order formula \(\varphi(\bar y)\) in the language \({<}\), there is a quantifier-free formula \(\psi(\bar y)\) such that

\[
T_{\mathrm{DLO}}\models \forall \bar y; \bigl(\varphi(\bar y)\leftrightarrow \psi(\bar y)\bigr).
\]
raw_proof_text:
It is enough to show how to eliminate a single existential quantifier from a quantifier-free formula. Once this is done, we eliminate quantifiers one at a time, starting from the innermost quantifier and proceeding outward.

Let (\theta(x,\bar y)) be quantifier-free. We will show that

\[
\exists x,\theta(x,\bar y)
\]

is equivalent over \(T_{\mathrm{DLO}}\) to a quantifier-free formula in the variables \(\bar y\).

First put \(\theta\) into disjunctive normal form:

\[
\theta(x,\bar y)\equiv \bigvee_{\alpha=1}^N \bigwedge_{\beta=1}^{M_\alpha} \ell_{\alpha,\beta}(x,\bar y),
\]

where each \(\ell_{\alpha,\beta}\) is a literal, meaning either an atomic formula or the negation of an atomic formula. Since existential quantification distributes over finite disjunctions,

\[
\exists x,\theta(x,\bar y)
\equiv
\bigvee_{\alpha=1}^N
\exists x,\bigwedge_{\beta=1}^{M_\alpha}\ell_{\alpha,\beta}(x,\bar y).
\]

Thus it suffices to eliminate \(x\) from a finite conjunction of literals.

In the language \({<}\), atomic formulas are of the form \(u<v\) or \(u=v\), where \(u,v\) are variables. Since the order is linear, negated atomic formulas can be rewritten positively:

\[
\neg(u<v)\quad\text{is equivalent to}\quad u=v\vee v<u,
\]

and

\[
\neg(u=v)\quad\text{is equivalent to}\quad u<v\vee v<u.
\]

After distributing finite disjunctions again, each conjunction can be reduced to a finite conjunction of positive atomic formulas of the form

\[
u<v
\quad\text{or}\quad
u=v.
\]

So we now consider a conjunction \(\chi(x,\bar y)\) consisting only of formulas \(u<v\) and \(u=v\).

If \(\chi\) contains an equality \(x=y_i\), then

\[
\exists x,\chi(x,\bar y)
\]

is equivalent to the formula obtained by substituting \(y_i\) for \(x\) everywhere in \(\chi\). This formula is quantifier-free in \(\bar y\). If \(\chi\) contains two equalities \(x=y_i\) and \(x=y_j\), then after substitution we also get the condition \(y_i=y_j\). If the equalities are inconsistent with some strict inequality, the resulting quantifier-free formula is simply false.

Thus we may assume that \(\chi\) contains no equality involving \(x\). Then all occurrences of \(x\) in \(\chi\) are inequalities. We can separate them into three types:

1. lower-bound inequalities
\[
   a_i(\bar y)<x \qquad (i=1,\ldots,m);
\]

2. upper-bound inequalities
\[
   x<b_j(\bar y) \qquad (j=1,\ldots,n);
\]

3. inequalities not involving \(x\), which we collect into a formula \(\eta(\bar y)\).

Here each (a_i(\bar y)) and (b_j(\bar y)) is one of the variables among \(\bar y\). Thus

\[
\chi(x,\bar y)
\equiv
\eta(\bar y)
\wedge
\bigwedge_{i=1}^m a_i<x
\wedge
\bigwedge_{j=1}^n x<b_j.
\]

We claim that over dense linear orders without endpoints,

\[
\exists x\left(
\bigwedge_{i=1}^m a_i<x
\wedge
\bigwedge_{j=1}^n x<b_j
\right)
\]

is equivalent to

\[
\bigwedge_{i=1}^m\bigwedge_{j=1}^n a_i<b_j.
\]

This convention also covers the cases \(m=0\) or \(n=0\): if there are no lower bounds, then the right-hand side has no conditions involving lower bounds; if there are no upper bounds, similarly.

We prove the claim. First suppose that there exists \(x\) such that

\[
a_i<x<b_j
\]

for every \(i,j\). Then by transitivity,

\[
a_i<b_j
\]

for every \(i,j\). Hence the right-hand side holds.

Conversely, suppose that

\[
a_i<b_j
\]

for every \(i,j\). We need to find \(x\) above all the \(a_i\) and below all the \(b_j\).

If there are no lower bounds and no upper bounds, then any element \(x\) works.

If there are lower bounds but no upper bounds, choose an element \(a_1\) among the lower bounds. Since there is no top endpoint, there exists \(x>a_1\). Repeatedly using linearity, choose \(x\) above the maximum of the finite list \(a_1,\ldots,a_m\). More explicitly, finite linear orders have a maximum among finitely many elements, and no endpoints gives a point strictly above that maximum.

If there are upper bounds but no lower bounds, the dual argument using no bottom endpoint gives \(x\) below all upper bounds.

Finally suppose there are both lower and upper bounds. Since the set \({a_1,\ldots,a_m}\) is finite and linearly ordered, it has a maximum \(a_{\max}\). Since \({b_1,\ldots,b_n}\) is finite and linearly ordered, it has a minimum \(b_{\min}\). The assumption \(a_i<b_j\) for all \(i,j\) gives

\[
a_{\max}<b_{\min}.
\]

By density, there exists \(x\) such that

\[
a_{\max}<x<b_{\min}.
\]

Then \(a_i\le a_{\max}<x\) for all \(i\), and \(x<b_{\min}\le b_j\) for all \(j\), so \(x\) satisfies all lower and upper bound constraints.

Thus the existential formula over \(x\) is equivalent to a quantifier-free formula in \(\bar y\). Combining this with \(\eta(\bar y)\), we have eliminated \(x\) from the conjunction \(\chi\).

Since every quantifier-free formula is a finite disjunction of such conjunctions, and since existential quantifiers distribute over finite disjunctions, every formula of the form \(\exists x,\theta(x,\bar y)\) with \(\theta\) quantifier-free is equivalent over \(T_{\mathrm{DLO}}\) to a quantifier-free formula in \(\bar y\).

Now take an arbitrary formula \(\varphi\). Put it into prenex or recursively eliminate innermost quantifiers: whenever a subformula has the form \(\exists x,\theta(x,\bar y)\) with \(\theta\) already quantifier-free, replace it by the quantifier-free formula constructed above. Universal quantifiers are handled by

\[
\forall x,\theta \equiv \neg \exists x,\neg\theta.
\]

By induction on the number of quantifiers, every formula is equivalent over \(T_{\mathrm{DLO}}\) to a quantifier-free formula. Therefore dense linear orders without endpoints admit quantifier elimination.
